coredata.dc:title: Age dependence of ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage in mice
coredata.dc:creator: Dalecki, Diane
Child, Sally Z.
Raeman, Carol H.
Cox, Christopher
Penney, David P.
Carstensen, Edwin L.
coredata.dc:description: Thresholds for ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage were determined in neonatal mice (24–36 h old), juvenile mice (14 d old) and adult mice (8–10 weeks old) to assess whether or not the threshold for lung hemorrhage is dependent upon age. Ultrasonic exposures were at 1.15 MHz with a pulse length of 10 μs, pulse repetition frequency of 100 Hz and a total exposure duration of 3 min. The threshold for lung hemorrhage occurred at a peak positive acoustic pressure of approximately 1 MPa for mice in all three age groups. Although the thresholds were similar for neonatal, juvenile and adult mice, the sizes of the suprathreshold hemorrhages were significantly larger in adult mice than in neonatal or juvenile mice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lung
Ultrasound
Hemorrhage
Neonatal mouse
Juvenile mouse
Bioeffect

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ELSEVIER Ultrasound in Med. & Biol., Vol. 23. No. 5, pp. 767-776, 1997 Copyright 1997 World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine & Biology Printed in the USA. All rights reserved 0301-5629197 $17.00 + .00 PH S0301-5629(97)00071-9 *Original Contribution AGE DEPENDENCE OF ULTRASONICALLY INDUCED LUNG HEMORRHAGE IN MICE DIANE DALECKI,* SALLY Z. CHILD,*CAROL H. RAEMAN,CHRISTOPHER COX,tDAVID P. PENNEY,fand EDWIN L. CARSTENSEN*Departments of *Electrical Engineering, tBiostatistics and *Pathology, and the ochester Center for Biomedical Ultrasound, University of Rochester, Rochester, NY (Received 24 June 1996; in final form 31 December 1996) Abstract-Thresholds for ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage were determined in neonatal mice (2436 h old), juvenile mice (14 d old) and adult mice (8-10 weeks old) to assess whether or not the threshold for lung hemorrhage is dependent upon age. Ultrasonic exposures were at 1.15 MHz with a pulse length of 10 its, pulse repetition frequency of 100 Hz and a total exposure duration of 3 min. The threshold for lung hemorrhage occurred at a peak positive acoustic pressure of approximately 1 MPa for mice in all three age groups. Although the thresholds were similar for neonatal, juvenile and adult mice, the sizes of the suprathreshold hemorrhages were significantly larger in adult mice than in neonatal or juvenile mice. C 1997 World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine & Biology. Key Words: Lung, Ultrasound, Hemorrhage, Neonatal mouse, juvenile mouse, Bioeffect. INTRODUCTION Many recent studies have demonstrated that exposure to diagnostic levels of pulsed ultrasound can produce lung hemorrhage (Baggs et al. 1996; Child et al. 1990; Frizzell et al. 1994; Harrison et al. 1995; Holland et al. 1996; Penney et al. 1993; Raeman et al. 1996; Tarantal and Canfield 1994; Zachary and O'Brien 1995). Currently, the adult mouse lung is the most thoroughly investigated and characterized biological model for ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage. The acoustic pressure threshold in situ for hemorrhage in the adult mouse lung is of the order of 1 MPa for exposure at 2 MHz with a 10-acs pulse length, 100-Hz pulse repetition frequency (PRF) and 3-min exposure time (Child et al. 1990). This threshold was found to be relatively independent of PRF and whether focused or unfocused fields were used for exposure (Child et al. 1990). An increase in carrier frequency from 13.7 MHz approximately doubled the threshold for lung hemorrhage (Child et al. 1990). Several studies suggest that the total ultrasound on-time and the exposure duration (on-time plus off-time) only weakly influence the threshold for lung hemorrhage. First, for a constant total on-time of 0.03 s of ultrasound, the threshold for ' Address correspondence to: Dr. Diane Dalecki, Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Rochester, Rochester, NY 14627, USA. a 3-min exposure (10-Ws pulse length, 17-Hz PRF) was approximately equal to the threshold for a 3-s exposure (10-fLs pulse length, 1000-Hz PRF) (Raeman et al. 1993). Second, no statistically significant difference in the threshold for lung hemorrhage was found at 2.3 MHz (10-as pulse length, 100-Hz PRF) for exposure times of 20 s (ultrasound on-time of 0.02 s ) or 3 min (ultrasound on-time of 0.18 s) (Raeman et al. 1996). Also, for exposures of 20 pulses (ultrasound on-time of - 20 ps) from an electrohydraulic lithotripter, the threshold for hemorrhage in the adult mouse lung is on the order of 1.5 MPa (Bailey et al. 1996; Hartman et al. 1990). These studies indicate that the threshold for hemorrhage in the adult mouse lung is relatively insensitive to total ultrasound on-time, although it should be noted that at suprathreshold exposures, the extent of hemorrhage does increase with exposure time (Raeman et al. 1996). Frizzell et al. (1994) studied lung hemorrhage in neonatal mice exposed to 1-MHz focused ultrasound. Using a 10-).s pulse length, they compared thresholds for exposures at a PRF of 1 kHz for 2.4 s (ultrasound on-time of 0.024 s) to exposures at a PRF of 100 Hz for 180 s (ultrasound on-time of 0.18 s). They reported thresholds for neonatal lung hemorrhage at peak negative acoustic pressures of 1.5 MPa and 0.37 MPa for exposure durations of 2.4 s and 180 s, respectively. Contrary to Raeman et al. (1996), these results indi- 767 768 Ultrasound in Medicine and Biology Volume 23, Number 5, 1997 cated that longer ultrasound on-times produce lower thresholds for lung damage. In addition, the threshold for neonatal lung hemorrhage for a 3-min exposure was approximately half of the threshold for lung hemorrhage in the adult mouse (Child et al. 1990). These results suggested that the lungs of neonatal mice may respond differently to ultrasound exposure than those of adult mice. The goal of this investigation was to determine if the threshold for ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage in the mouse is dependent on age. Acoustic pres sure thresholds were determined for lung hemorrhage in neonatal (24-36 h old), juvenile (14 d old) and adult (8-10 weeks old) mice. The results indicate that the thresholds for the three age groups were essentially the same. EXPERIMENTAL METHODS The acoustic source used in this study was a 1.15MHz focused transducer fabricated from a 1-inch diameter piezoceramic element cemented to a plano-con cave aluminum lens having a focal length of 6.5 cm. The acoustic field was measured daily using a bilaminar, PVDF membrane hydrophone (Marconi Research Center, Chelmsford, UK) calibrated by comparison to a sphere radiometer (Dunn et al. 1977). Calibrations were performed in a tank of degassed, deionized water maintained at the temperature used for exposure (see below). In addition to peak positive and maximum negative acoustic pressures that could be read directly from the hydrophone traces, spatial peak, pulse average intensities were obtained by integrating the square of the measured pressures over the duration of the pulse using a digital oscilloscope (LeCroy Model 9400, Chestnut Ridge, NY, USA) and dividing the result by the characteristic impedance of water and the pulse length (10 ps). For convenience and uniformity, all of the results of this study are reported in terms of the peak positive acoustic pressure. However, Table 1 gives the corresponding negative acoustic pressures and spatial peak, pulse average intensities for each Table 1. Acoustic field parameters in water [peak positive acoustic pressure p+ (MPa), peak negative acoustic pressure p_ (MPa) and spatial peak pulse average intensity ISPPA (W cm 2); frequency: 1.15 MHz]. p+ P_ 15PPA (MPa) (MPa) (W cm 2) 0.5 0.5 6.6 1.0 0.9 24 1.5 1.2 50 2.0 1.5 85 2.5 1.8 124 3.0 2.0 166 exposure level at the surface of the animal. The -6 dB beam width at the focus was 3.6 mm. Adult C3H male and female mice were obtained from Jackson Laboratories (Bar Harbor, ME, USA). For mating, two females were placed with one male at approximately 3:00 p.m. until approximately 10:00 a.m. the following morning. Vaginal plugs were considered indicative of successful mating. Pregnant females were housed no more than four per cage through gestation. For the following experiments, neonatal mice were aged 24-36 h and juvenile mice were 14 d old. All experimental protocols were approved by the University of Rochester Committee on Animal Resources. Neonates Two separate studies were performed to determine the threshold for lung hemorrhage in neonatal mice. In the first study, mice were exposed at 10 to replicate the exposure protocol of Frizzell et al. (1994). At 10, the metabolic rate of the neonates is slowed and they were not observed to be breathing. A second study was performed with exposures at 37. These two studies will be referred to as the cold neonate and warm neonate studies. For the cold neonate study, each animal was placed on ice for approximately 5 min to immobilize the animal, and exposures were performed in a water bath maintained at 10. For the warm neonate study, each neonate was first immobilized with ether, and exposures were performed in a 37 water bath. The remainder of the experimental protocol was common to both the cold neonate and warm neonate studies. Following immobilization by either ice or ether, the neonate was rolled in a layer of plastic wrap (Saran Wrap Dow Brands L.P., Indianapolis, IN, USA) from shoulders to tail to aid in securing the animal to the holder. The region of plastic wrap over the right lung was cut away for exposure. The neonate was then secured to a Plexiglas holder (Fig. la) for ultrasound exposure to the dorsal aspect of the animal. The torso of the neonate was located over a 2-cm-diameter hole in the Plexiglasholder. The nose and mouth of the neonate were inside a thin plastic tube containing trapped air for breathing during exposure. The lower third of the neonate was secured to the holder using surgical tape. The holder with the neonate was placed in a tank containing degassed, deionized water. The right lung was aligned with a pointer in the water tank using a three-way positioner. The coordinates from the pointer to the focus of the acoustic beam were measured daily with a membrane hydrophone. Using these coordinates, the neonate was positioned so that the right lung was at the focus of the acoustic beam. To determine the threshold for lung hemorrhage, neonates were exposed to peak positive acoustic pres- Age dependence of ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage D. DALECKi et al. 769 Fig. 1. Holders for exposure of (a) neonatal and (b) juvenile mice. The thin plastic tubes contain air and allow breathing during the exposure period. sures of 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5 and 3.0 MPa. All exposures used a 10-lcs pulse length at 100-Hz PRF for a total exposure time of 3 min. Sham-treated neonates experienced the same experimental protocol as exposed neonates except they were not irradiated with ultrasound. Immediately following exposure, neonates were removed from the water tank and sacrificed by decapitation with a scalpel. Limbs were pinned to a natural rubber block to secure the animal for dissection. The upper back was then cut away to expose both lungs for inspection. At this point, while still in the chest cavity, lungs were scored for hemorrhage using a IOX inspection microscope. Upon gross inspection, hemorrhages were observed as distinct areas of red lesions. The major and minor axes of the oval hemorrhage were measured and areas were computed as an ellipse. The scorer was always blind to the exposure conditions. Juveniles Fourteen-day-old C3H mice were anesthetized with ketamine (200 mg kg-) and rompun (10 mg kg-'). Their backs were shaved and depilated with Neetto minimize trapped air on the skin surface. Each juvenile was secured to the plastic holder depicted in Fig. lb. The nose and mouth were inside the thin plastic tube used for breathing, and the limbs were secured to the cork areas using h-pins. The holder containing the juvenile was then attached to a three-way positioner and placed in a tank of degassed, deionized water at room temperature (27'C). The left lung was positioned at the acoustic focus by using a pointer in the tank, and coordinates obtained through hydrophone measurements as described above. Twenty juvenile mice were exposed randomly to each of the following exposure levels: sham, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0 and 3.0 MPs peak positive acoustic pressure. All exposures were at 1.15 MHz with a pulse length of 10 /is, PRF of 100 Hz, and total exposure time of 3 min. Immediately following exposure, animals were killed by cervical dislocation. The upper back was opened to access the lungs. The lungs were then removed from the chest cavity and scored for hemorrhage. The investigator scoring for hemorrhage was always blind to the exposure conditions. Attenuations of the dorsal rib cages of juvenile mice were measured to obtain an estimate of the acoustic field at the surface of the lung. Anesthetized juve nile mice (n = 3) were first cervically dislocated. Then the chest was opened and the heart and lungs removed. An Imotec PVDF needle hydrophone (Imotec Gmbh, Wurselen, Germany) was placed inside the chest cavity and positioned at the acoustic focus. Transmission measurements through the rib cage of the mouse were performed by comparing peak positive acoustic pressures measured by the hydrophone with and without the tissue in the field. Using a three-way positioner, the tissue was translated relative to the stationary hydrophone to obtain several measurements through intercostal spaces. Neonatal mice were too small to measure, and attenuations through the rib cages of adult mice were measured previously (Child et al. 1990). Adults Adult, 8- to 10-week-old, C3H mice obtained from Jackson Laboratories were anesthetized and irradiated using the same procedures and exposure levels as described for juvenile mice. Exposures were performed in a tank of degassed, deionized water at room temperature (27'C). The small animal holder used for adult mice did not require any breathing tube since it was possible to maintain the head above the water level with the adult mouse. Following exposure, animals were cervically dislocated and scored as in the juvenile protocol. Statistical analyses Two qualitatively different approaches were used to analyze the observations of hemorrhage. In one case, 770 Ultrasound in Medicine and Biology Volume 23, Number 5, 1997 the mean lesion areas were the basis of the analyses. In the other case, the data were simply the fraction of animals in each exposure group with lesions. The first approach contains information on the extent of damage. The second approach is based on incidence of hemorrhage. Because qualitatively different data and statistical models are used in the analyses, thresholds in the two cases can be different. In both cases, thresholds for ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage were estimated using the linear plateau or "hockey stick" regression model (Baggs et al. 1996; Dalecki et al. 1997a,b). This model assumes a constant level of background response (unrelated to ultrasound exposure) until the threshold is reached. After the threshold is exceeded, the level of response is assumed to increase linearly with increasing exposure. For data in the form of rates (i.e., percentage of lungs with hemorrhage), a plateau of 100% response is eventually achieved. For continuous data (i.e., area of hemorrhage), a plateau is not usually assumed. In summary, the model estimates three parameters from the data; these are the background level of response, the threshold and the slope of the response above threshold. For data in the form of fraction of lungs with hemorrhage, these three parameters were estimated using the method of maximum likelihood (Cox 1984). For data in the form of area of hemorrhage, the parameters were estimated by nonlinear regression analysis. These techniques provide a statistically optimal estimate of the three parameters of the model. RESULTS Certain problems unique to neonates had to be solved before arriving at the experimental protocol described above. The neonatal lung was far more suscep tible to damage from handling than adult lung. When we removed neonatal lungs following the procedure that we had used for adults, the sham hemorrhage rate was unacceptably high. That difficulty led to the procedure described above where the tissues over the lung were removed without manipulating the lung itself and scoring was performed in situ. Only the dorsal surface of the lung was visible for inspection, but that was the surface proximal to the source. In addition, the lungs of all of the cold neonates took on a mottled appearance that made scoring difficult. This mottled, pseudohemorrhagic appearance was not unique to neonates. Adult mice held at 10 for 10 min showed the same appearance (see below). When neonates were held at 37, their lungs took on the uniform pink color that is characteristic of adults at room temperature or 37. Results of the cold neonate study are shown in the second column of Table 2, which presents the number of animals with lung hemorrhage per number of Table 2. Numbers of animals with lung hemorrhage per number of animals exposed for each acoustic exposure condition. P+ Cold neonates Warm neonates Juveniles Adults (MPa) Sham 3/20(6/20) 3/20 1/20 0/10 0.5 1/20 4/20 1/20 0/10 1.0 7/20(4/20) 5/20 1/20 2/10 1.5 3/20 9/20 12/20 9/10 2.0 4/20 15/20 15/20 10/10 2.5 7/20 - - - 3.0 8/20 19/20 20/20 10/10 Acoustic pressures are at the surface of the animal. The ratios in parentheses for the cold neonates are the results of the follow-up test of whether the original 1-MPa datum could be considered an outlier. animals exposed for each exposure condition and each age group. Lungs were scored as positive only if there appeared a distinct red region of hemorrhage. For the cold neonate protocol, the diffuse red mottling was scored as negative for hemorrhage. The first study, where 20 cold neonates were randomly irradiated at each exposure condition, produced a datum at 1 MPa (7/20) that was somewhat greater than observed at either higher or lower pressures, suggesting that it may have been an outlier. To test the statistical hypothesis that the high rate of hemorrhage observed at 1.0 MPa in the first study was an outlier, a second experiment was performed to determine whether the rate of hemorrhage for 1.0-MPa exposures was significantly different than for sham exposures. In this study, an additional 20 neonates were exposed at 1.0 MPa, and 20 neonates were sham-treated as detailed in the cold neonate protocol of the Experimental Methods. In this study, the percentages of neonates with lung hemorrhage were 20% for exposures at 1.0 MPa and 30% for shamtreated neonates, as shown in the parentheses of Table 2. These results support the hypothesis that the original 1-MPa data point in the cold neonate study was an outlier. A summary of the sizes of lung hemorrhages for the cold neonate study is shown in Table 3. Areas of lung hemorrhage are presented in terms of two means. M, is the mean area of lung hemorrhage only for animals with hemorrhage. M, describes the size of a hemorrhage when it occurs and may appear misleading if an exposure condition has a single large hemorrhage. Mz is the mean area of lung hemorrhage for all exposed animals and includes 0-mm' areas for animals without hemorrhage. MZ reflects both the size and the frequency of occurrence of hemorrhage. Cold neonates showed a high rate of spontaneous hemorrhage in sham-exposed animals. In addition, the MZ for exposures at 3 MPa is not greater (statistically) than Mz for any of the lower exposure conditions. As a result, Age dependence of ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage 0 D. DALECxi et al. 771 Table 3. Summary of lung hemorrhage sizes for cold neonates. P+ No. animals M, area No. animals MZ area (MPa) with hemorrhage (mm2) [SE] exposed (mm') [SE] Sham 9 7.23 [3.521 40 1.63 [0.90] 0.5 1 0.39 [0.001 20 0.02 [0.02] 1.0 1 1 2.37 [1.051 40 0.59 [0.39] 1.5 3 1.37 [0.35] 20 0.21 [0.16] 2.0 4 1.47 [0.29] 20 0.30 [0.181 2.5 7 3.12 [0.22] 20 1.09 [0.761 3.0 8 1.03 [0.36] 20 0.14 [0.18] M, is the mean area of lung hemorrhage for animals with hemorrhage. MZ is the mean area of lung hemorrhage for all exposed animals and includes 0-mm' areas for animals without hemorrhage. Data in the brackets are standard errors [SE]. Acoustic pressures are at the surface of the animal. no clear threshold is obtainable for the threshold for lung hemorrhage in cold neonates for the exposures in this study. In striking contrast, the results for the warm neonates (column 3 of Table 2) showed a relatively low rate of hemorrhage in sham exposures while 95% of the animals exposed at 3 MPa had lung hemorrhages. A summary of the mean areas of lung hemorrhages for the warm neonates is shown in Table 4. A clear threshold for lung hemorrhage can be determined. At the highest exposure, the average area of lung damage was approximately 5 mmZ. Figure 2 shows a plot of the mean area of hemorrhage (MZ) vs. exposure condition and illustrates that both the "threshold" for hemorrhage and the size of hemorrhage are drastically different in warm neonates than cold neonates. The incidences of lung hemorrhage in juvenile mice are shown in column four of Table 2 and hemorrhage sizes are given in Table 5. The rate of hemor rhage in sham exposures was only 5% and, at the highest exposure, all animals showed lung hemorrhage. Data summarizing the results of the adult mouse exposures are given in column five of Table 2 and in Table 6. None of the sham-exposed adult mice showed any hemorrhage while all mice exposed at 2.0 and 3.0 MPa had lung hemorrhage. Thresholds at the surface of the lung can be estimated by accounting for attenuation loss through the rib cage of the mouse. In this study, the minimum attenuation through the juvenile mouse rib cage was measured to be 0.4 dB (SD = 0.06). Child et al. (1990) measured a minimum attenuation of 1.5 dB for the adult mouse rib cage. Neonatal mice were too small to perform attenuation measurements. The mean area of hemorrhage (MZ) is shown as a function of exposure condition in Fig. 3 for exposures of warm neonates, juveniles and adults. For an appro priate comparison among age groups, it is necessary to express the data in terms of acoustic pressure at the surface of the lung. The pressures in the tabular data, therefore, have been reduced by 0 dB for neonates, 0.4 dB for juvenile mice and 1.5 dB for adults. Estimated thresholds in MPa (95% confidence limits) at the surface of the lung were 1.35 (1.15, 1.55) for warm neonates, 1.24 (1.12, 1.35) for juveniles and 1.05 (1.02, 1.07) for adult mice. An F test for the significance of the differences among the three thresholds was marginally significant (F(2,9) = 4.41, p = 0.046). This weak trend of decreased threshold with increasing age when the data are analyzed in terms of lesion area, even though it has borderline statistical significance, is not supported by the data when analyzed in terms of rate of incidence, as discussed in the next paragraph. The data on incidence of lesions are presented in Fig. 4. Estimated thresholds (SD) at the surface of the lung were 0.63 (0.28) MPa for warm neonates, 0.92 (0.08) MPa for juveniles and 0.72 (0.10) MPa for adults. The likelihood ratio test for difference of the three thresholds was negative (x2 = 1.74 with 2 df, p = 0.42). Applying the model to all of the incidence data under the assumption of a common threshold yields a threshold estimate of 0.75 (0.07) MPa at the surface of the lung. Figure 4 shows the analysis under this assumption of a common threshold. The 95% confidence interval for the combined threshold extends from 0.61-0.90 MPa. Figure 2 demonstrates that the response of neonatal lungs exposed at 10 is significantly different than lungs exposed at 37. Neonates maintained at 10 were not observed to breath and often turned blue in color. In an effort to explain the striking difference in response of the lungs of warm and cold neonates to pulsed ultrasound, we tested the effects of temperature, gas content of the lungs and blood flow on the sensitivity of adult murine lungs. Each experiment used the 1.15-MHz focused source described in the Experimental Methods with a 10,us pulse length and 100-Hz PRF. Table 4. Summary of lung hemorrhage sizes for warm neonates. P+ No. animals M, area No. animals MZ area (MPa) with hemorrhage (mmZ) [SE] exposed (mmZ) [SE] Sham 3 1.57 [0.78] 20 0.24 [0.16] 0.5 4 1.19 [0.671 20 0.24 [0.16] 1.0 5 0.75 [0.16] 20 0.19 [0.08] 1.5 9 1.20 [0.30] 20 0.54 [0.19] 2.0 15 3.10 [0.561 20 2.33 [0.52] 3.0 19 5.29 [0.751 20 5.02 [0.761 M, is the mean area of lung hemorrhage for animals with hemorrhage. MZ is the mean area of lung hemorrhage for all exposed animals and includes 0-mm' areas for animals without hemorrhage. Data in the brackets are standard errors [SE]. Acoustic pressures are at the surface of the animal. 772 Ultrasound in Medicine and Biology Volume 23, Number 5, 1997 " 7E 54 0 E = 47 cts ar a 32 1 0 , 1 r ~i rr ... I r-r-^r-r i i-rr-I i 7 0 0.5 1 1.5 2 2.5 Peak Positive Pressure (MPa) 0 Cold Neonates O Warm Neonates ,& Juveniles Fig. 2. Comparison of lung hemorrhage in cold neonates vs. warm neonates. Cold neonates were exposed at 10, warm neonates were exposed at 37 and juveniles were exposed at room temperature (27). Results of cold neonates are significantly different than both warm neonates and juveniles. Error bars are standard errors. In a direct test of the effect of body temperature on the extent of lung hemorrhage, adult mouse lungs were exposed in either a 10, 27 or 37 water bath. A thermocouple was placed in either the thigh or abdomen to determine when the body temperature equilibrated with the bath temperature. After equilibration, mice were exposed at 3.3 MPa for 1 min (10-ps pulse length, 100-Hz PRF, 1 MHz). Figure 5 shows the mean lesion area after an exposure at each temperature. No dependence of lung hemorrhage on temperature was observed. The reduced respiration and blue color of neonates at 10 suggested a possible increased level of C02 in the lungs and blood. To test the effect of increased levels of C02 on lung hemorrhage, adult mice were exposed after breathing C02 through a cone placed over the nose and mouth. In condition #1, ultrasound exposure commenced after breathing C02 for 30 s. In condition #2, exposure began after breathing C02 for 2.5 min at which time breathing was observed to cease. Figure 6 summarizes the results of these experiments. A final experiment tested directly the importance Table 5. Summary of lung hemorrhage sizes for juveniles. Table 6. Summary of lung hemorrhage sizes for adults. P+ No. animals M, area No. animals M2 area P+ No. animals M, area No. animals M2 area (MPa) with hemorrhage (mm2) [SE] exposed (mm2) [SE] (MPa) with hemorrhage (mm2) [SE] exposed (mm2) [SE] Sham 1 3.93 [0.00] 20 0.20 [0.20] Sham 0 0.00 [0.00] 10 0.00 [0.00] 0.5 1 0.79 [0.00] 20 0.04 [0.04] 0.5 0 0.00 [0.00] 10 0.00 [0.00] 1.0 1 0.79 [0.00] 20 0.04 [0.04] 1.0 2 0.79 [0.00] 10 0.16 [0.10] 1.5 12 2.45 [0.48] 20 1.47 [0.40] 1.5 9 6.35 [1.07] 10 5.71 [1.19] 2.0 15 3.77 [0.51] 20 2.83 [0.53] 2.0 10 20.74 [2.44] 10 20.74 [2.44] 3.0 20 8.41 [1.23] 20 8.41 [1.23] 3.0 10 44.69 [5.15] 10 44.69 [5.15] M, is the mean area of lung hemorrhage for animals with hemorrhage. M2 is the mean area of lung hemorrhage for all exposed animals and includes 0-mm2 areas for animals without hemorrhage. Data in the brackets are standard errors [SE]. Acoustic pressures are at the surface of the animal. M, is the mean area of lung hemorrhage for animals with hemorrhage. M2 is the mean area of lung hemorrhage for all exposed animals and includes 0-mm2 areas for animals without hemorrhage. Data in the brackets are standard errors [SE]. Acoustic pressures are at the surface of the animal. Age dependence of ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage D. DALECKI et al. 773 0 a c 0 m 0.0 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 Acoustic Pressure Amplitude (MPa) Fig. 3. Mean areas of lung hemorrhage (mm') for warm neonates (circles, solid line), juveniles (diamonds, dotted line) and adults (triangles, dashed line). The abscissa is the peak positive acoustic pressure at the surface of the lung. Bars are standard errors. m os 0 E 0.6 S e 0.4 3 0.2 1.0 0.8 0.0 I 0.0 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 Acoustic Pressure Amplitude (MPs) Fig. 4. Probability of lung hemorrhage for warm neonates (circles, solid line), juveniles (diamonds, dotted line) and adults (triangles, dashed line). These are data from the same experiments used in Fig. 3, except that the ordinate is percentage of animals with hemorrhage of total animals exposed (Table 2). The abscissa is the peak positive acoustic pressure at the surface of the lung. Bars are standard deviations. 774 Ultrasound in Medicine and Biology Volume 23, Number 5, 1997 of blood flow in the expression of lung hemorrhage. In this study, adult mice were first killed by cervical dislocation and the heart was observed to continue beating for several minutes. After 10 min, when the heart had stopped beating, the lung was exposed for 3 min at 3 MPa. Figure 7 compares the extent of hemorrhage for these animals with living animals. DISCUSSION Lung hemorrhage in adult mice is a strong function of the amplitude of the acoustic pressure in the incident pulse. Hemorrhage in sham-exposed adult mice is rare. Thus, it is possible to extrapolate suprathreshold exposures to zero incidence or zero area and obtain threshold pressures for lung hemorrhage. Using different measures of hemorrhage, three independent studies have given roughly the same value of the threshold for lung hemorrhage in adult mice exposed at 1 MHz. In terms of the peak positive pressure at the surface of the lung, the values were 0.7 MPa (Child et al. 1990), 1.1 MPa (Raeman et al. 1993) and the two threshold values of 1.0 and 0.7 MPa from this study. A closer look at these values demonstrates the Gas Condition 10 27 37 Temperature ( ) Fig. 5. Effects of bath temperature on the sensitivity of adult murine lung to pulsed-ultrasound exposures. In each case, the exposures were 3.3-MPa peak positive pressure at the surface of the animal for 1 min using the 1.15-MHz focused source with a 10-ps pulse length and 100-Hz pulse repetition frequency. Bars are standard errors. Fig. 6. Effects of CO2 on the sensitivity of adult murine lung to pulsed-ultrasound exposures. Hemorrhage area in mice breathing air (n = 3) compared to mice breathing COz. In condition #1 (n = 3), ultrasound exposure began after breathing COZ for 30 s, while in condition #2 (n = 3), exposure began after breathing COZ for 2.5 min. In each case, the exposures were at room temperature at 3.3-MPa peak positive pressure for 3 min using the 1.15-MHz focused source with a 10-ps pulse length and 100-Hz pulse repetition frequency. Bars are standard errors. dependence of the threshold value on the model chosen to analyze the data. Analysis of the data on the basis of incidence of hemorrhage led to the two threshold values of 0.7 MPa. Analysis on the basis of lesion area led to the values of 1.0 and 1.1 MPa. Thresholds obtained from lesion area and incidence data may represent qualitatively different biological endpoints. In any event, the differences between the results of the two approaches are small. The models are useful primarily as simple descriptors of data, and when comparing experiments, the dependence of the descriptors on the model should be considered. In contrast, the report of a threshold as low as 0.3 MPa for neonatal mice (Frizzell et al. 1994) opened the possibility that young animals may be more sensi tive to ultrasound than adults. In addition to general developmental considerations, neonatal mice are relatively inactive and their lungs still contain some fluid. However, 14-d-old juvenile mice are much more active and their lungs should be free of amniotic fluid. This study was undertaken primarily to test the hypothesis Age dependence of ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage D. DALECttt et al. 775 Fig. 7. Effect of reduced blood flow on the sensitivity of adult murine lung to pulsed-ultrasound exposures. In the "dead" condition (n = 3), mice were first cervically dislocated and then exposed to ultrasound 10 min later when the heart was not observed to be beating. In each case, the exposures were at room temperature at 3.3-MPa peak positive pressure for 3 min using the 1.15-MHz focused source with a 10-Fts pulse length and 100-Hz pulse repetition frequency. Bars are standard errors. that juvenile mice and adult mice will respond similarly to ultrasound exposure but under the tacit assumption that neonates would be different. Although adult mice had been studied earlier, a threshold study was conducted again on adult mice, as well as neonatal and juvenile mice, to be certain that exposures and scoring procedures were the same for each age group in the comparison. The surprising result of the study was the finding that warm neonates, juveniles and adults had thresholds for lung hemorrhage that were the same within the reliability of the data. For neonates, we initially followed the procedures used by Frizzell et al. (1994) as closely as possible. We found, as they did, that hemorrhage in sham-exposed neonates was high. Our results at 10 were qualitatively similar to those reported by Frizzell et al. (1994 ) in the sense that sham scores were high. However, only 40% of the neonatal lungs exposed to 3 MPa at 10 showed lung hemorrhage, and these hemorrhages Normal Dead Blood Flow Condition were usually < 1 mm2 in area. Most important, at 10, the mean lesion area for exposures at 3 MPa was not different statistically than for sham-exposed lungs, and a clear threshold for lung hemorrhage could not be obtained. These atypical results appear to be related to the physiological state rather than the age of the animals. When the bath temperature was increased to 37, scores for hemorrhage in sham-exposed animals dropped, 95% of neonatal lungs exposed to 3 MPa at 37 showed > 5 mm' areas of lung hemorrhage and a well-defined threshold for hemorrhage was measured at approximately 1.3 MPa. In view of the overall picture of lung hemorrhage at higher temperatures summarized in Figs. 3 and 4, it does not appear warranted to conclude that the threshold for lung hemorrhage in mice is a function of age. The striking difference in response of the lungs of neonatal mice to ultrasound at 10 and 37 is probably directly related to blood flow in the animals. Neonatal mice do not have a robust thermoregulatory system. When held at 10, respiratory activity essentially stops and cardiac rate probably is very low. Frizzell et al. (1994) took advantage of this characteristic to avoid the use of chemical anesthesia in their studies of lung hemorrhage in neonates. Adults continue to breath and may be assumed to have nonzero blood flow even at 10. Exploratory tests on adults suggest that the differences in response of warm and cold neonates cannot be directly attributed to temperature. Anesthetized adults were placed in a 10 water bath and, when temperatures measured with a thermocouple in the abdomen reached bath temperature, the lungs were exposed to pulsed ultrasound. The degree of hemorrhage was not significantly different than that observed at room temperature. Upon autopsy, the lungs of adult mice that had been held at 10 had the same mottled appearance that had been seen in cold neonates. It appears, therefore, that it is the effect of temperature on blood flow in the neonates rather than temperature itself that reduces the apparent effectiveness of ultrasound in cold neonates. Figure 6 shows that breathing carbon dioxide also reduces the apparent damage of ultrasound on the lung. Table 7. Average size of neonatal, juvenile, and adult lung. Age n Mean length Mean width Mean area group (mm) (mm) (mm2) Neonates 5 8.0 [0.71 3.8 [0.31 23.9 Juveniles 6 10.8 [1.51 5.2 [0.81 44.1 Adults 12 13.6 [1.01 6.6 [0.91 70.5 Areas were computed by approximating the lung as an ellipsoid. n is the number of animals, and data in brackets are standard deviations. 776 Ultrasound in Medicine and Biology Volume 23, Number 5, 1997 But, this again appears to result directly from the effect of the gas on respiration rate, heart rate and hence blood flow. The final test of the requirement of blood flow to demonstrate lung hemorrhage is shown in Fig. 7. If ultrasound causes any damage to lung tissue, it is very difficult to detect by macroscopic inspection unless the heart continues to function during the exposure. The results of this study suggest that the threshold for lung hemorrhage in mice is independent of age. But the surprising discovery was that suprathreshold exposures produced greater damage to lung tissue in adults than in younger animals. This was true both for the absolute size of lesions produced and in the relative fraction of the lung surface with hemorrhage. Table 7 provides approximate measurements of whole murine lung at the ages investigated in this study. Areas of the lung were computed by approximating the lung as an ellipsoid. Even when the areas of hemorrhage (Tables 4-6) are normalized for the size of the lung, the percentage of lung hemorrhaged in adults is approximately three times greater than for juveniles or neonates: at 3 MPa, the percentage of lung damaged was 21% for warm neonates, 19% for juveniles and 63% for adults. The limited data available at the present time suggest that not only is the threshold for ultrasound-induced lung hemorrhage independent of age, but the threshold also appears to be independent of species. Baggs et al. (1996) reported an in situ threshold of 1.1 MPa for lung hemorrhage in neonatal swine exposed to 2.3-MHz pulsed ultrasound (10-ps pulse length, 100Hz PRF) in a 39 water bath. The corresponding threshold for lung hemorrhage in 10-d-old swine was found to be 0.8 MPa (Dalecki et al. 1997b). These values are very similar to the threshold found for mice at the same frequency (Raeman et al. 1996). The thresholds reported for rats (Holland et al. 1996) and mice (Child et al. 1990) at 4 MHz also are similar. Acknowledgements-The authors are indebted to Professor Leon Frizzell, Bioacoustics Research Laboratory, University of Illinois, for guidance in the initial phases of the neonatal studies and for comments on the final manuscript. This work was supported in part by USPHS Grant No. DK39796. REFERENCES Baggs R, Penney DP, Cox C, et al. Thresholds for ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage in neonatal swine. Ultrasound Med Biol 1996;22:119-128. Bailey MR, Dalecki D, Child SZ, et al. Bioeffects of positive and negative acoustic pressures in vivo. J Acoust Soc Am 1996; 100:3941-3946. Child SZ, Hartman CL, Schery LA, Carstensen EL. Lung damage from exposure to pulsed ultrasound. Ultrasound Med Biol 1990;16:817-825. Cox C. Generalized linear models-The missing link. Appl Stat 1984;33:18-24. Dalecki D, Raeman CH, Child SZ, et al. Hemolysis in vivo from exposure to pulsed ultrasound. Ultrasound Med Biol 1997a; 22:307-313. Dalecki D, Child SZ, Raeman CH, Cox C, Carstensen EL. Ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage in young swine. Ultrasound Med Biol 1997b; 23. Dunn F, Averbuch AJ, O'Brien WD Jr. A primary method for the determination of ultrasonic intensity with the elastic sphere radiometer. Acustica 1977;38:58-61. Frizzell LA, Chen E, Lee C. Effects of pulsed ultrasound on the mouse neonate: Hind limb paralysis and lung hemorrhage. Ultrasound Med Biol 1994;20:53-63. Harrison GH, Eddy HA, Wang JP, Liberman FZ. Microscopic lung alterations and reduction of respiration rate in insonated anesthetized swine. Ultrasound Med Biol 1995;21:981-983. Hartman C, Child SZ, Mayer R, Schenk E, Carstensen EL. Lung damage from exposure to the fields of an electrohydraulic lithotripter. Ultrasound Med Biol 1990;16:675-679. Holland CK, Deng CX, Apfel RE, et al. Direct evidence of cavitation in vivo from diagnostic ultrasound. Ultrasound Med Biol 1996;22:917-925. Penney DP, Schenk EA, Maltby K, et al. Morphologic effects of pulsed ultrasound in the lung. Ultrasound Med Biol 1993; 19:127-135. Raeman CH, Child SZ, Carstensen EL. Timing of exposures in ultrasonic hemorrhage of murine lung. Ultrasound Med Biol 1993;19:507-512. Raeman CH, Child SZ, Dalecki D, Cox C, Carstensen EL. Exposuretime dependence of the threshold for ultrasonically-induced murine lung hemorrhage. Ultrasound Med Biol 1996;22:139-141. Tarantal AF, Canfield DR. Ultrasound-induced lung hemorrhage in the monkey. Ultrasound Med Biol 1994;20:65-72. Zachary IF, O'Brien WD Jr. Lung hemorrhage induced by continuous and pulsed wave (diagnostic) ultrasound in mice, rabbits, and pigs. Vet Pathol 1995;32:43-54.

head.ce:title: Age dependence of ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage in mice

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Rochester, Rochester, NY, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biostatistics, University of Rochester, Rochester, NY, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pathology, University of Rochester, Rochester, NY, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rochester Center for Biomedical Ultrasound, University of Rochester, Rochester, NY, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Address correspondence to: Dr. Diane Dalecki, Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Rochester, Rochester, NY 14627, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thresholds for ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage were determined in neonatal mice (24–36 h old), juvenile mice (14 d old) and adult mice (8–10 weeks old) to assess whether or not the threshold for lung hemorrhage is dependent upon age. Ultrasonic exposures were at 1.15 MHz with a pulse length of 10 μs, pulse repetition frequency of 100 Hz and a total exposure duration of 3 min. The threshold for lung hemorrhage occurred at a peak positive acoustic pressure of approximately 1 MPa for mice in all three age groups. Although the thresholds were similar for neonatal, juvenile and adult mice, the sizes of the suprathreshold hemorrhages were significantly larger in adult mice than in neonatal or juvenile mice.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thresholds for ultrasonically induced lung hemorrhage in neonatal swine

Direct evidence of cavitation in vivo from diagnostic ultrasound
Morphologic effects of pulsed ultrasound in the lung

Exposure-time dependence of the threshold for ultrasonically-induced murine lung hemorrhage
Ultrasound-induced lung hemorrhage in the monkey
Lung hemorrhage induced by continuous and pulsed wave (diagnostic) ultrasound in mice, rabbits, and pigs
